Unit 2.6 Planning Fallacy
• What’s planning fallacy?
• Mechanisms underlying planning fallacy
• The inside–outside model (neglect of past experiences +
narrow focus on plans for successful task completion)
• Conjunctive-events and disjunctive-events bias
• Debiasing strategies for planning fallacy
3

PLANNING FALLACY
• Consider the following classic example of planning fallacy:
In 1976, Daniel Kahneman was involved in a project to develop a curriculum for
a new subject area for high schools in Israel. In a meeting, individual team
members’ estimation on completion time ranged from 18 to 30 months.
Try to recall as many curriculum development
Not all the teams that I can think of have
teams as you can. Think of them as they were in a

stage comparable to ours at present. How long did
eventually gave up. Of the remaining,
it take them at that point to reach completion?
completion time ranged from 7 to 10 years.
Any reasons to believe that our
No. Indeed, my impression is that
team is more skilled in curriculum
we are slightly below average in
development than the earlier ones?
terms of resources and potential.

4

• Planning fallacy is the tendency to underestimate the resources (time and
money) needed for project completion, despite that the vast majority of similar
projects have failed to proceed as planned.

Optimism about
the future

The average time overrun of IT projects Students have ample experiences
in U.S. was 222% . finishing their assignments later than
(The Standish Group, 1995)
originally planned.

.
(Buehler, Griffin & Ross, 1994, Study 1)

about their new infrastructure projects.
6

MECHANISMS UNDERLYING THE PLANNING FALLACY
When predicting task completion, we should derive our predictions from both
(i) singular information – aspects of the particular task at hand, and
(ii) distributional information – the distribution of outcomes for similar tasks, e.g.,
your own past experiences (personal distributional information) or past
experiences of others (populational distributional information).
Factors that prevent us from generating an accurate prediction:
• The inside–outside model (neglect of past experiences + narrow focus on plans
for successful task completion)
• Conjunctive-events and disjunctive-events bias
7

The Inside–Outside Model
The inside–outside model of planning fallacy claims that people rely far more on
singular information (inside view) than on distributional information (outside
view) when estimating the completion time / resources needed of a task.
People tend to generate their predictions by considering the unique features of
the task at hand and constructing a scenario of their successful task completion,
i.e., to develop a series of steps that will lead from the beginning to a successful
conclusion of the project. Doing so results in reduced attention to past experience
and future impediments.
Let’s examine:
1. Why people fail to incorporate past experience (distributional information) in
their completion estimates?
2. Why the resulting focus on singular information is dominated by plans for
successful task completion (vs. obstacles)?
8

Despite the predictive value of past experience (distributional information),
we neglect them while forming prediction. Why might this be so?
1. The forward nature of prediction: The act of prediction, by its very nature,
elicits a focus on the future rather than on the past; a future orientation may
prevent individuals from looking backward in time.
2. Attributional processes that diminish the relevance of past failures to the
present: To the extent that people perceive a previous failure to be caused
by external, unstable, and specific factors, they need not connect the past
failure to future occasions.
9

Consider the following study (in which think-aloud procedure was used):
• First, participants (UG students) described a school project that they planned to
complete in the next 2 weeks and its deadline. Then were asked to (i) predict as
accurately as possible when they would finish the project, and (ii) indicate how
certain they were about the predicted time.
• Second, participants were asked to recall (i) one occasion in which they had failed
to complete a task by the time they originally predicted and a similar prediction
failure experienced by a friend or acquaintance, then (ii) explain why each of the
two tasks was not finished by the expected time.
• Participants were interviewed 1 week after the project deadline to record the
actual completion time.
Predicted completion date Verbal protocols on
Actual
+ Verbal protocols on attribution about (i) own
completion
prediction + Confidence prediction failure &
date
ratings on prediction (ii) others’ prediction failure
10

• Results:
Predicted vs. actual completion times:
On average, participants reported they were 83.5% certain that they would
complete the project in the predicted time. But only 43.6% of the participants
finished their projects within the predicted time.
Verbal protocols on prediction:
A 7-category system was developed to characterize the content of participants’
verbal protocols as they generated their prediction. For each participant, the
proportion of responses assigned to each category was calculated. (Please see next
slide for information on those categories.)
Consistent with the inside–outside model, participants (i) focused more on future
scenarios than past experiences, and (ii) thought more about plans for future
progress than potential impediments.
11

Category Proportion Example
Future plans: Participants’ plans for .71 "Let's see, I have the morning off, I have classes in the
completing a project and thoughts about afternoon, I have to do some fieldwork in the late
its likely progression. afternoon, so it will probably be done about 7pm."
Future problems: Participants’ .03 "I don't know. It might take a bit longer because I'm not
references to potential impediments. quite caught up in this."
Past success: References to projects that .06 "I'll put about five hours, that's about my average for all
were completed as anticipated. of the assignments."
Past problems: References to problems .01 "It took me days and days and days to do a term paper
or impediments encountered in the past. last year for Social Work."
Others’ experience: References to other .01 "I think it's more time than most people are spending
people's experiences. on it, but I have to weigh all of the information given to
me in this project."
Disposition: Participants’ personal .02 "I'm very slow and I'm a perfectionist, especially as far
characteristics. as art is concerned."
Deadline .15 "Well, the due date is July 12 at 5 o'clock. I'll probably
finish the project half an hour before it's due."
12

Verbal protocols on attribution:
A set of 7-point scales was developed to rate the attributions along 3 dimensions:
The extent to which the cause (i) implicated other people and circumstances or the
person themselves (external vs. internal), (ii) was temporary or persistent
(transitory vs. stable), and (iii) was specific to the particular project or relevant to
many different activities (specific vs. global).
Comparing to attributions offered for others’ prediction failures, participants’
attributions for their own prediction failures were more external, transitory, and
specific (e.g., “my delay was an accident” vs. “my friend failed to get the work done
as predicted because he is a procrastinator”), i.e., participants made use of
attributional mechanisms to discount the relevance of past failures.
Buehler, R., Griffin, D., & Ross, M. (1994). Exploring the ‘‘planning fallacy’’: Why people underestimate their task completion
times. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 67, 366–381. Study 3.
13

Why is the inside view (singular information) dominated by plans for
successful task completion (vs. possible obstacles or interruptions)?
Motivated reasoning is a form of reasoning in which people construct arguments
in a biased fashion to arrive at a preferred conclusion (i.e., a conclusion that
people are motivated to draw).
In the domain of planning, our goal / motivation to finish tasks quickly enhances
the accessibility of information that is consistent with the desired goal, this leads
to a greater focus on successful future plans and a reduced focus on potential
obstacles.
14

Consider a study on the role of motivated reasoning in planning fallacy:
• Participants were asked to complete a series of word puzzle tasks:
1st word 2nd word Manipulation 3rd word
puzzle task puzzle task of motivation puzzle task
Time
• After the first two tasks were completed, the motivation manipulation were
introduced:
Speed incentive condition: US$2 reward for finishing 1 min faster than the
previous tasks and US$4 reward for finishing 2 mins faster.
No incentive condition: Simply being told to finish as quickly as possible.
• Participants then (i) predicted how long it would take to complete the 3rd task,
(ii) wrote down thoughts that came through their minds when making the
prediction, and (iii) completed the 3rd task.
15

• Results:
Predicted vs. actual completion times:
Participants in the speed incentive (vs.
no incentive) condition predicted shorter
completion times, i.e., suffered more from
planning fallacy.
Verbal protocols on prediction:
A 4-category system was developed to
characterize the content of participants’ verbal
protocols.
- Using past: Using past tasks as the basis for
predicting their completion time (e.g., “My first
time was longer than the second, so I chose a
time half way between them”).
16

- Discounting past: Considered the past tasks but explained why the upcoming task
would be different from the previous ones (e.g., “I looked at my last time score but
decided that I would probably do better because of practice”).
- Future planning: Plans and strategies for completing the upcoming task (e.g., “Next
time I’ll look for letter E”).
- Future impediments: Problems or impediments that might be encountered with the
upcoming task (e.g., “The next set of words might be more difficult”).
Participants in the speed incentive (vs. no incentive) condition generated
(i) fewer thoughts about using the past as a basis for prediction, (ii) more thoughts
designed to deny the relevance of the past tasks, (iii) more thoughts about future
plans or strategies, and (iv) fewer thoughts about potential problems.
Buehler, R., Griffin, D., & MacDonald, H. (1997). The role of motivated reasoning in optimistic time predictions. Personality and
Social Psychology Bulletin, 23(3), 238-247. Study 2.
17

Conjunctive-events and Disjunctive-events Bias
Conjunctive-events bias: People tend to
overestimate the probability of conjunctive
events (the “AND events”).
E.g., You are trying to catch Flight 1, which Flight 1
Flight 2
you have 30% chance of getting on it, and
a connecting Flight 2, which you have 30%
chance of getting on it. What is the
probability of making both flights?
• While people anchor their predictions The probability of the intersection is
too high (near 30%), the actual likelihood the product of the components:
of making both flights is 9%. p(F1 AND F2) = p(F1)*p(F2)
18

Disjunctive-events bias: People tend to
underestimate the probability of disjunctive
events (the “OR events”).
Flight 1
E.g., You can take either Flight 1 (which
Flight 2
you have 30% chance of getting on it), or
Flight 2 (which you also have 30% chance
of getting on it). What is the probability
that you can make the trip?
• While people anchor their predictions p(F1 OR F2)
= p(F1) + p(F2) - p(F1 AND F2)
too low (near 30%), the actual likelihood
= p(F1) + p(F2) - p(F1)*p(F2)
of making a flight is 51%.
19

Conjunctive-events and disjunctive-events bias offers an explanation for planning
fallacy: When constructing future scenarios of multifaceted projects, we tend to
underestimate the likelihood that our plans might go wrong.
• E.g., A building can only be completed on time if there are no delays in the
delivery of materials, no strikes, no shortages of funds during construction, no
unusual weather conditions, and so on. Although any one of these setbacks
may be unlikely, the probability of at least one of these setbacks occurring is
considerably more so.
20

DEBIASING THE PLANNING FALLACY
Discussion:
Let’s suppose you’re a programmer, and you’re developing a computer game. To
complete the game development, you have to design a mockup, complete the
main scene (play button, help button), game scene (score display, sound effects, object
movement), win scene (“you win”, fireworks animation), lose scene (“game over”, restart
button), help scene (text, back button).
Your boss asked you to estimate when the computer game will be ready so that
he can announce the product launch date accordingly.
Given the mechanisms of planning fallacy (e.g., neglect of past experiences,
narrow focus on plans for successful task completion, conjunctive-events and
disjunctive-events bias), what can you / your boss do to reduce planning fallacy
(and thus the negative consequences associated with it)?
21

Let’s examine some examples of debiasing strategies:
1. Use past distributions as the basis for prediction.
• To reduce planning fallacy, decision makers should base their prediction on
actual performance in a reference class of comparable projects (distributional
information) – Reference class forecasting.
• E.g., In a software company, programmers were significantly underestimating
the amount of time it would take them to complete various tasks, leading to
missed deadlines and burnout. Rather than trying to change the
programmers’ estimates, the company accommodated the planning fallacy by
adding “buffer time” to projects. The company looked at historical data on
work completion dates to determine how much buffer time to add to each
project, such as 20% for a minor software update or 40% for a more complex
operating system update.
22

2. Use unpacking to reveal the complexity (potential impediments) of the task.
Consider an experiment:
• Participants were given 9 unformatted text (9 definitions adapted from a
dictionary), and were asked to edit the text using MS Word so that it looked
exactly like the properly formatted version.
• Participants were asked to unpack the task into subcomponents, i.e., to list
each and every specific step that would be needed in order to complete the
formatting task such as “adding italics”, “adding punctuation”, and “capitalizing
letters”.
Kruger, J., & Evans, M. (2004). If you don't want to be late, enumerate: Unpacking reduces the planning fallacy. Journal of
Experimental Social Psychology, 40(5), 586-598. Studies 3 and 5.
23

• 2 experimental conditions:
Packed
Time prediction Unpack the task Complete the task
condition
Unpacked
Unpack the task Time prediction Complete the task
condition
• Results:
Mean predicted and actual Unpacking a whole project into
formatting completion times (mins) subcomponents resulted in less optimistic
50 predictions. The subtasks serve as reminders
Predicted Actual
of steps that need to be completed, but
40
would have otherwise been unattended in
30 the rosy future scenarios.
20 Further study showed that the influence of
unpacking is greater for complex task than
10
for simple task.
Packed Unpacked
24

Intended Learning Outcomes For Unit 2.6 Time For Reflection
Recall an experience in which
By now, you should be able to:
you’ve exhibited planning
• Define planning fallacy, and discuss its impacts in
fallacy. How would you
organizational context and in your personal life.
prevent it from happening
• Describe the mechanisms underlying planning fallacy,
again in the future?
and then suggest ways to debias.
25

actual performance in a reference class of comparable projects (distributional
information) – Reference class forecasting.
• E.g., In a software company, programmers were significantly underestimating
the amount of time it would take them to complete various tasks, leading to
missed deadlines and burnout. Rather than trying to change the
programmers’ estimates, the company accommodated the planning fallacy by
adding “buffer time” to projects. The company looked at historical data on
work completion dates to determine how much buffer time to add to each
project, such as 20% for a minor software update or 40% for a more complex
operating system update.
22

2. Use unpacking to reveal the complexity (potential impediments) of the task.
Consider an experiment:
• Participants were given 9 unformatted text (9 definitions adapted from a
dictionary), and were asked to edit the text using MS Word so that it looked
exactly like the properly formatted version.
• Participants were asked to unpack the task into subcomponents, i.e., to list
each and every specific step that would be needed in order to complete the
formatting task such as “adding italics”, “adding punctuation”, and “capitalizing
letters”.
Kruger, J., & Evans, M. (2004). If you don't want to be late, enumerate: Unpacking reduces the planning fallacy. Journal of
Experimental Social Psychology, 40(5), 586-598. Studies 3 and 5.
23
